FT_IC1.evidence: Longitudinal data from the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) study, 10,317 participants, baseline 2016-2018, 21 U.S. recruitment sites, ages 9-10 at baseline, 4.0 data release.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis using longitudinal logistic regression models examining associations between ACEs (baseline through Year 2) and cyberbullying victimization at Year 2.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article in Journal of Adolescence (2023); default presumption of non-duplicate applies.

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1: All numeric estimates in Tables 2 and 3 pair ACE scores/types with cyberbullying victimization. No phone/SM-specific variable (texting time, social media time, SMAQ, MPIQ, etc.) appears as IV or DV with its own coefficient anywhere in the paper, including supplementary tables. Step 2 not reached. Cyberbullying alone does not qualify per binding clarification #3 — cyberbullying fails IC2 unless paired with a separately-measured phone/SM variable that has its own coefficient.

notes: Paper is entirely about ACEs -> cyberbullying victimization. Screen time is mentioned only narratively in Discussion as a potential mediating pathway (citing Jackson et al., 2021) but no screen time variable is analyzed or reported with a coefficient.

FT_IC1.evidence: Cross-sectional data from Year 2 of the ABCD Study (4.0 release), 9,443 adolescents, 21 U.S. recruitment sites, conducted 2018-2020.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical cross-sectional analysis using modified Poisson regression to examine associations between cyberbullying and sleep outcomes.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article in Academic Pediatrics (2023); default presumption of non-duplicate applies.

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1: All main inferential analyses (Table 3, Models 1-3) pair cyberbullying victimization/perpetration (IV) with sleep outcomes (DV). Total recreational screen time appears only as a covariate in Model 3, not as an IV or DV with its own primary analysis. No phone/SM-specific variable appears as IV or DV with its own coefficient anywhere. The screen time variable ('sum of eight different screen modalities on a typical weekday and weekend') is a multi-device composite used as covariate only. Step 2 not reached. Cyberbullying alone fails IC2 per binding clarification #3; screen time as covariate-only also fails IC2 per binding clarification #7.

notes: Total recreational screen time (8-modality composite including TV, gaming consoles, etc.) appears in Model 3 as a covariate alongside sociodemographics. Its RR is reported (RR 1.02 per hour) but it appears only as a control variable in models where cyberbullying is the IV and sleep is the DV. Screen time is not the focal IV or DV of any analysis.

FT_IC1.evidence: Cross-sectional data from Year 2 of the ABCD Study (4.0 release), N=10,339, 2018-2020, ages 10-14 years, 21 U.S. recruitment sites.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical cross-sectional analysis using multiple linear regression to estimate associations between sexual minority identification and screen use outcomes.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article in Annals of Epidemiology (2023); default presumption of non-duplicate applies.

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1 finds qualifying estimates: Table 2 reports sexual minority identification (IV) associated with social media hours (B=0.46, 95% CI 0.25-0.67, p<0.001), texting hours (B=0.64, 95% CI 0.33-0.96, p<0.001), video chat hours (B=0.24, 95% CI 0.04-0.43, p=0.014), SMAQ score (B=0.46, 95% CI 0.32-0.60, p<0.001), and MPIQ score (B=0.56, 95% CI 0.41-0.70, p<0.001). Social media time, texting time, video chat time, SMAQ, and MPIQ are all qualifying phone/SM-specific variables. Sexual minority identification (sexual orientation) is an individual-trait variable explicitly classified as such under v7.

FT_IC3.evidence: Multiple linear regression analyses with B coefficients and 95% CIs reported throughout Tables 2 and 3 for the sexual orientation -> SM/phone use relationships.

FT_IC4.evidence: Numeric estimates (B coefficients, 95% CIs, p-values) reported for all SM/phone-specific outcomes in Tables 2 and 3.

FT_EC7_reason: Design is cross-sectional (Year 2 ABCD data only); FT-EC7 requires a longitudinal design as a precondition. EC7 does not apply.
notes: Sexual orientation -> screen use study. Phone/SM-specific variables (social media hours, texting hours, SMAQ, MPIQ) are the DVs; sexual minority status is the IV. Sexual orientation is individual-trait per v7 explicit classification. Paper also reports VGAQ scores and gaming hours, but those do not affect the IC2 decision since qualifying SM/phone estimates are present in Step 1.
